english: gyúr-čig (gyur cig) (= bčúg-nas (bcug nas), or bčúg-ste (bcug ste)) may I, after having been buried alive, be obliged to eat my own son's flesh! Dzl.; usually however employed in the minor clauses of accessory sentences: brós-śiṅ °gáb-pas (bros shing 'gab pas) having hid themselves after running away Dzl.; frq. also where coordinate ideas are in English connected by and or but: śá-la zá-żiṅ krág-la °túṅ-ba (sha la za zhing khrag la 'thung ba) eating flesh and drinking blood; čé-żiṅ légs-pa (che zhing legs pa) tall and well-shaped; drod gnód-čiṅ bsil-ba phaṅ (drod gnod ching bsil ba phang) heat is hurtful (but), cold is beneficial Lt. It is also used like the ablative of the gerund in Latin: nya bśór-żiṅ °tsó-o (nya bshor zhing 'tsho 'o) we live by fishing (piscando) Dzl.; and = kyiṅ (kying) (q.v.): rí-la draṅ-sroṅ byéd-čiṅ °dúg-go (ri la drang srong byed ching 'dug go) he sits on the mountain acting the part of an anchorite Dzl.; smre-sṅágs °dón-čiṅ °dug (smre sngags 'don ching 'dug) he sits wailing Dzl.; raṅ-dgár gró-żiṅ yda (rang dgar gro zhing yda) he is wandering at pleasure Mil.; čes smrá-żiṅ yód-pa-la (ces smra zhing yod pa la) as they were thus speaking Glr.; čos stón-čiṅ yód-pai tse (chos ston ching yod pa'i tshe) as he was just giving religious instruction Tar. 11, 12.
n. of a female demon Thgr.

termin. of či (ci), 1. whereto etc., little used. — 2. with yaṅ (yang): everywhere, in every direction, for any purpose, by all means, with a negative: nowhere (so at least it is to be explained in several passages of Pth. and Thgy.).

instr. of či (ci), čis yid-čes-par gyur (cis yid ches par gyur) by what am I to believe it? what shall make me believe it? whereby can I know it to be true? Dzl.; čis kyaṅ mi skrág-pa yin (cis kyang mi skrag pa yin) he is not to be frightened by any thing Dzl.; čis kyaṅ (cis kyang), and či-nas kyaṅ (ci nas kyang) frq. used as adv.; by all means, at all events, at any rate, čis kyaṅ gró-na (cis kyang gro na) if you wish to go by all means, at all hazards; da čis kyaṅ gegs byao (da cis kyang gegs bya'o) now I will at any rate play him a trick Dzl.; čis kyaṅ bżes-pa żu (cis kyang bzhes pa zhu) I beg of you most earnestly to accept it Mil.; čis kyaṅ slobs (cis kyang slobs) never mind! teach it me at any rate! Pth.

1. num. figure: 65. — 2. inst. of bču (bcu), used in compound numerals for the tens, when the preceding numeral ends with a consonant: sum-ču (sum cu), drug-ču (drug cu), bdun-ču (bdun cu), brgyad-ču (brgyad cu).
Med., Cs.: 'a sort of lime used for medicine'.
(? yču-ti (gcu ti)) pig-tail, cue, worn by boys and men in Tibet proper, Ld. and Sp. Cf. čo-to (co to).

1. W. a fresh apricot. — 2. C. dried apricots without stones. — 3. a sort of wild-growing vegetable Sik., C. — ču-li ta-gir (cu li ta gir) the pulp of apricots boiled down to a conserve and formed into cakes W. — bun-ču-li (bun cu li) a kind of peach Kun.

1. C. gourd, pumpkin. — 2. n. of a place. — 3. for čuṅ żig (cung zhig): da kyod čuṅ phýis-pa yin (da khyod cung phyis pa yin) you are a little too late now Pth.; čuṅ yó-ba (cung yo ba) a little slanting Glr.

wylie: cung zhig, cung zad, cung zad
english: a little, B. and C., mu-ge čuṅ-zad čig (mu ge cung zad cig) a partial famine Mil.; čuṅ-zad-kyi pýir (cung zad kyi phyir) for the sake of a trifle, through an insignificant circumstance Dzl. 227, 15; some, Lat. nonnulla, of rare occurrence, Was. (242); čuṅ-żig skyéṅ-bar gyúr-nas (cung zhig skyeng bar gyur nas) rather ashamed, somewhat confounded Glr.; čuṅ-żig pán-nam blta (cung zhig phan nam blta) I shall see, whether it will help, or has helped, a little Mil.; a little while, a short space of time, čuṅ-zad čig sdod čig (cung zad cig sdod cig) wait a little (while) Dzl. When followed by a negative, it may either be translated as in: čuṅ-zad ma bdé-ba (cung zad ma bde ba) a little unwell, uneasy etc. Mil., or as in: dbaṅ čuṅ-zad med (dbang cung zad med), there is not even a slight possibility = there is no possibility at all Pth. and elsewh.
also čuṅ-żu (cung zhu), čuṅ-żi (cung zhi), yčoṅ-żi (ycong zhi), a kind of white stone.
W., from the Hind. चुप, *čub čád-de dug* (*cub cad de dug*) he keeps silence, holds his peace.
in čur míd-pa (cur mid pa) to devour food entire Sch.
